Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd usually wait until I had a chunk of free time, which at 27 with life responsibilities doesn't come as regularly as it did when I was younger. I tried to dedicate around 23 hours a week, but it was more like cramming sessions when I could fit them in rather than any organized schedule. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to lectures while doing other things around the house.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and whatever else was going on in my life. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, other weeks I'd be up late trying to catch up on everything I'd missed. I don't have the luxury of a fixed schedule anymore—work and personal stuff always seemed to take priority, and studying filled in the gaps.
Q3: Usually it was an assignment deadline looming or realizing I'd missed several classes and needed to figure out what was happening in the course. Sometimes I'd get an email reminder about something due, and that would prompt me to log in. It wasn't really a habit—more like damage control when I remembered I was actually enrolled in this course.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were maybe a few weeks early on where I tried to make it a regular thing, but it never stuck. Life just kept getting in the way, and the LMS felt like one more thing demanding my attention. The gamified tasks they introduced were kind of interesting, but even that novelty wore off quickly for me.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I could listen to them and actually absorb the information—that's just how my brain works. I watched over 200 minutes of video content because hearing someone explain concepts is way more effective for me than reading dense text. When professors spoke clearly and gave examples, I felt like I was actually learning something.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion boards after a while. I only posted maybe seven times the whole course, and I really only interacted with one other student. It felt performative and time-consuming for not much payoff. The readings I'd often skim or skip entirely if I could get the gist from the lectures instead.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what I needed for the assignments or exams. If I could see a clear purpose—like this will help me pass—then I'd engage with it. Anything that felt like busywork or extra fluff, I just couldn't justify spending energy on when I had so many other demands on my time.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required ones I'd force myself to complete because my grade depended on it, but optional stuff? I'd look at it and think, 'That's nice, but I have bills to pay and a life to manage.' There was always this mental calculation of effort versus reward, and optional activities rarely won that battle.
Q9: Absolutely. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I also took handwritten notes that I'd review later, completely offline. Sometimes I'd find YouTube videos or podcasts that explained the same concepts better than the course materials, and I'd use those instead.
Q10: There were plenty of times I was actively learning but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like I'd download a PDF once, print it out, and spend hours marking it up and studying from the physical copy. Or I'd be replaying a lecture in my head while walking the dog. My actual study time was way more than what my login frequency would suggest.
Q11: All the time. I'd access something once, download it, and then never go back to the LMS for that material again. It was more efficient for me to have everything saved locally so I could study whenever and wherever without needing internet access. The LMS was basically just a delivery system for getting materials onto my own devices.
Q12: Around midterms, everything got harder. My stress levels were already medium, and adding exam pressure on top of work responsibilities made me want to just check out completely. My attendance dropped significantly during those periods—I was only at about 34% for the whole course. When things piled up, the course was the first thing I let slide.
Q13: Definitely. Fatigue was a big one—after a long day, the last thing I wanted to do was log into some platform and engage with coursework. Competing responsibilities constantly pulled me away. When I was overwhelmed, I'd do the bare minimum on the LMS just to stay afloat, even if I knew I should be doing more.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be logged in, clicking through materials, but my brain was completely elsewhere. I'd watch a video and realize at the end I hadn't absorbed a single thing because I was stressed about something else entirely. It was like going through the motions without actually being present.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because they felt like the least important thing. I knew participating mattered, but when I was anxious about bigger assignments or exams, the discussions were easy to push aside. I'd tell myself I'd do them later, and then later never came until it was too late.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to engage consistently. I'd see a due date approaching and suddenly find the motivation to log in and get things done. My assignment completion was actually pretty high at 86% because I'd always rally at the last minute. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have done much at all.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got good feedback, it was a nice boost but didn't really change my behavior. When feedback was critical, it sometimes made me more anxious and less likely to engage because I'd start doubting myself. I needed more time to process feedback emotionally before I could use it productively.
Q18: The structure felt pretty rigid, which didn't work great for someone like me who needed flexibility. The gamified elements they introduced were a nice attempt to make things more engaging, but honestly, at my age and with my situation, I needed practical relevance more than game mechanics. The structure assumed students had consistent time to dedicate, which I didn't.
Q19: Having video content available was a huge help for my learning style. But the emphasis on discussion participation made things harder because I'm not naturally inclined to post my thoughts publicly, especially when I was barely keeping up with the basics. The peer interaction expectations felt unrealistic given how disconnected I already felt from the course.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for what they need, clicking around trying to understand the material, but struggling to actually produce work. Or maybe they're anxious and keep checking things without feeling confident enough to submit. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just spinning wheels.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. My LMS activity was pretty low, but I still passed and did okay on assignments because I was studying in ways that didn't show up in the system. I think some of us just learn better outside of these platforms and only use them when absolutely necessary. The logs don't capture everything.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show whether you actually understood anything or how much effort you put into offline studying. They miss the mental engagement, the stress you're carrying, the life circumstances affecting your participation. It's a very surface-level picture of what's really happening.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting genuine mental effort into understanding it—not just completing tasks to check boxes. For me, engagement happened most when I was listening to lectures and things clicked, even if I wasn't actively participating in discussions or logging in frequently. It's an internal thing more than an external performance.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I'm a 27-year-old trying to balance education with real-world responsibilities, or that my moderate anxiety sometimes paralyzed me from participating even when I wanted to. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with downloaded materials, or how the gamified tasks felt patronizing rather than motivating at my age. My engagement was real, just not very visible.
